content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
107 SOUTH BROADWAY • • • SUITE 7007 • • • LOS ANGELES, 90012

April 23, 1981

A-81-169

Ms. Barbara Myers
RIVERSIDE COUNTY ELECTIONS DIVISION
4175 Main Street
Riverside, California 92501

Dear Mrs. Myers:

This letter is to confirm my advice to you during our
telephone conversation of Wednesday, April 22, 1981 concerning
the costs incurred for a feasibility study and processing fees
paid to the Local Agency Formation Commission (LAFCO).

The facts as I understand them are that group has hired
an independent consulting firm to conduct a feasibility study
concerning the incorporation of a designated area, pay the processing fee to LAFCO, and, file the necessary petitions required by
the California Government Code with LAFCO. The Local Agency
Formation Commission reviews the proposal, formulates its own
feasibility study and submits a resolution or recommendation
to the County Board of Supervisors. The Board of Supervisors
then makes the decision whether or not to place the issue on
the ballot for the people to vote on.

The question you asked was whether or not the costs
relating to the feasibility study conducted by the consulting
firm, the processing fee paid to LAFCO and the costs relating
to the petitions filed with LAFCO are reportable under the
Political Reform Act?

After discussing the above facts with our Commission's
Chief Legal Counsel, Mr. Bob Stern, I advised that the costs
for the feasibility study, petitions and processing fee paid
to the local agency formation Commission were not reportable
under the Political Reform Act as "expenditures."

Section 18225 of the California Administrative Code,
in part, defines "expenditure" made for "political purpose"
as any payment "made for the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or against....the
qualification.... of any measure." Since the issue is not

• SACRAMENTO (916) 322-5660 • LOS ANGELES (213) 620-5196

Ms. Barbara Myers
April 23, 1981
Page Two

determined by the consulting firm nor the LAFCO as to whether
or not the issue is to be placed on a ballot to be voted upon
by the general public, all costs related to this part of the
process of incorporation or annexation does not qualify as being
for "political purpose." The general public is not being asked
to either vote for or against any measure, nor sign any petition
concerning a referendum or initiative.

However, once the Board of Supervisors decides to place
the issue before the public on a ballot, all monies received and
spent relating to the ballot measure are required to be disclose
and periodic campaign statements filed.

I hope this information is helpful to you. Please do not
hesitate to contact our office if we can be of any further
assistance.

Sincerely,

Helen P. Arriola
Political Reform
Consultant

HPA:jg

sections.question: Whether or not the costs relating to the feasibility study conducted by the consulting firm, the processing fee paid to LAFCO and the costs relating to the petitions filed with LAFCO are reportable under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion: The costs for the feasibility study, petitions and processing fee paid to the Local Agency Formation Commission were not reportable under the Political Reform Act as "expenditures" because the issue is not determined by the consulting firm or LAFCO as to whether or not the issue is to be placed on a ballot. However, once the Board of Supervisors decides to place the issue before the public on a ballot, all monies received and spent relating to the ballot measure are required to be disclosed and periodic campaign statements filed.
sections.question_synthetic: Are costs for a feasibility study, LAFCO processing fees, and petition filing expenses related to a potential incorporation reportable as political expenditures under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that feasibility study costs, LAFCO processing fees, and petition filing expenses are not reportable expenditures under the Political Reform Act because they do not yet constitute payments to influence voter action. However, once the Board of Supervisors places the measure on a ballot, all related monies must be disclosed and campaign statements filed.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Clear advice letter with identifiable question and conclusion sections. Minor OCR issues (e.g., 'disclose' appears split) but content is clear. Document dated April 23, 1981, addressed to Ms. Barbara Myers at Riverside County Elections Division.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether or not the costs relating to the feasibility study conducted by the consulting firm, the processing fee paid to LAFCO and the costs relating to the petitions filed with LAFCO are reportable under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: The costs for the feasibility study, petitions and processing fee paid to the Local Agency Formation Commission were not reportable under the Political Reform Act as "expenditures" because the issue is not determined by the consulting firm or LAFCO as to whether or not the issue is to be placed on a ballot. However, once the Board of Supervisors decides to place the issue before the public on a ballot, all monies received and spent relating to the ballot measure are required to be disclosed and periodic campaign statements filed.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Riverside County Elections Division regarding whether costs for an incorporation feasibility study, LAFCO processing fees, and petition filing are reportable political expenditures. The FPPC concluded these costs are not reportable until the Board of Supervisors places the measure on a ballot.
